item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In addition to the consistencies everyone is pointing out, I don't like how the briefcase's output waffles between accurate information and silly nonsense. It read like the author couldn't decide what tone to pick, or what exactly this item should be.
Reading the discussion, I understand where this comes from, though, with the implications of too useful an object.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article couldn't be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept does not interest me, also, the article contradicts both itself and its test log.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could have been an interesting concept; but seeing as how this page is bare, and the execution in the supplement primarily involves silly pseudo-accurate origin stories for popular series 1 skips, it's a definite -1 on both this page and the supplement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description is contradictory—is the content of the documents "always factual" or "sometimes completely false"? If there's a limit of 1000 pages, then why do several of the experiments list examples with multiple thousands of pages (and why is that number given as "number of documents")? -1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good article, but would something like "living organism" be more clinical than " living thing?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somewhere in here, there may be a glimmer of a good idea and concept, but the article is wracked with contradictions and it makes it so uninteresting and confusing as to render reading it pointless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The above post contains all my gripes. Big enthusiastic downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is very unclear. It seems to be constantly contradicting itself. At first it explains how the documents' content is always printed in the same format and style, but then it claims that this same content appears to be random and that no pattern has been found.

Similarly, it first claims that "the content of these documents are not always accurate, sometimes being completely false", which is confirmed by stating that the documents occasionally mix in elements of fiction (though that also contradicts the previous claim that the contents are always presented in the form of a biography) and making false claims of the person being present at various world events, but then everything claimed up to that point is contradicted by stating that "Information about the subject has always been factual" (and indeed the log confirms that is NOT always factual).

Also, the log confirms that the 1000 pages thing is a maximum and not an exact number, but the way it's worded in the article, it sounds like it's saying that it can only produce that exact number. Also, the log shows that on two occasions, the briefcase produced over 9000 (no meme intended) pages, so that's yet another contradiction.

Speaking of the log, it comes with its own share of confusion:

- For the piece of basalt, did it somehow take 943 pages to explain that it contained a large dose of polonium? Or did it contain other information that was equally false (since the validity is "fully inaccurate")? And if that's the case, how can we tell the documents were even referring to the basalt?

- For the first doctor it's presented to, the validity is "Partially/Fully Accurate, Questionable Accuracy". Which is it?! It can't be all of those or even two of those at the same time.

- For SCP-914, the validity is "Fully Accurate - I think." No. That doesn't happen. A scientist, let alone an SCP Foundation scientist, does not "think" something is fully accurate. Either they can verify it or they find it questionable.

- For D-88322, the validity is "I need help filling this". Cute but no. Again, they would just say "questionable accuracy".

Also, I need to find a way to make my posts shorter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Down vote. This seems more like an anomalous item. The experiment logs don't help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Speaking of necro, I fixed a couple of minor typos in the text. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh. How do these mesh:

In spite of this, the content of these documents are not always accurate, sometimes being completely false.

Information about the subject has always been factual, and very few documents have omitted any confirmed factual accounts.

I get that it sometimes just makes up stuff, but the latter bit implies that embedded in the BS is an accurate history, while the former explicitly says it can be completely false. I haz a confused.

(Sorry for the necro!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i really like ones like this. +1 for collab logs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't sold until I read the logs. Love the random nature of it and how you're never quite sure with some of the more plausible ones…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gives a biogaphy that's not even accurate.
Feels more like an anomalous item instead of an actual SCP.

item.title: misused word
item.content:encoded:
The mechanism by which SCP-1402 achieves this is unknowns, as is the mechanism by which the object ascertains the paper and ink in which to print the information.

To ascertain is to learn something, as in "I ascertained her true motives." I think the the word you want to use is acquires.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uggg it was ruined by adding dumb downsides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't like the concept (both versions of it), but the execution was improved enough for me to cancel my downvotes, so, good work, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would rather like that, thank you.
Edit: After taking Reject's advice, I am now making 1402 to sometimes be inaccurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not voting because, while it's not bad, it doesn't do anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Before you even ask, you should rewrite this. +5 is not a solid rating for an SCP you're trying to get a lot of supplementary material on.

So. I'm volunteering myself to help you. PM me on the wiki or through chat and we can do this, if you'd like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sounds like a plan to me.
How might a collab be put to senior staff vote?
Should I simply message the senior staff about it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NO DAMNIT, WE'RE DOING THIS!

Well actually, we'll first need to put it to a Senior Staff vote. But it's been a while since we've had a good collaborative testing log, and having at least one in the 1000s Series (unless I'm forgetting something) would be nice.

Still need to do the whole misinformation thing, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A collab you say?
Like SCP-261?
Because if youse guys wanna do that, I would be honored.
Edit: Really, right now I'm starting to consider the experiment log to be rather unnecessary, and I've figured out that I could probably get across it's usefulness and it's harmfulness in just two addendum logs. But if anyone wants to actually collaborate, then I will most certainly let you guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. This.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ That sounds like it could make the Experiment Log more interesting.
- (HEY GUYS LET'S DO ANOTHER COLLABORATION).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it better that they do, because then the SCP is not constricted. Example:

Date: ██/██/20██
Presented to: SCP-682
Number of documents: 1000
Document content: The story tells a presumably fictional account of a young anomaly named Billy, orphaned at a young age but raised by his cruel sister and her thoughtful husband. One day, his best friend Able and him decided to make a heavy metal band called "Impenetrable Lizard." They toured together for many years, eventually adding a singer named Lilith and guitar player named Joe Perry. Their band was disbanded in 2001 after Able was killed in an attack on his mansion in Pentagon City, Virginia. Billy's home was invaded by MTF-Alpha One and he was imprisoned by the Foundation and branded "SCP-682." Attached documents after this point have confirmed factual accounts of SCP-682 in containment, including full test logs and containment breach incident reports.
Note: What the fuck did I just read?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My objection to 1402 isn't that it's simply useful. It's that in it's current form it's basically anathema to everything that makes SCP work. What makes an SCP interesting or scary is that they exhibit unexplained phenomenon and have an implicit mystery behind them. It's the world of analytical thinking and sequential logic desperately trying to manipulate, control and understand things that are inherently unknowable and uncontrollable. I'm the first to groan at the "Be vague because vague is scary!!!" school of writing, but when you explicitly explain everything about where the object came from and how it works then the power it has over you is lost. It's like explaining a joke, it'll make sense, but it will no longer be funny.

It doesen't matter that whoever wants to use 1402 still has to write the backstory themselves, they're still delivering far too much backstory and neutralizing the impact of the SCP in the process. Add on to this the fact that "a briefcase that we don't understand the underlying principles or origins of, told us everything about the underlying principles and origins of <SCP>" is quite possibly the most unsatisfying way to unravel a mystery, and you have concept poison.

Despite this, I still haven't downvoted because I actually like the idea, and it's so close to being decent, but without limits it can revolutionize the Foundation's understanding overnight. Yeah, sure, there is no canon and no one has to use 1402 if they don't want to, but as long as it exists the questions going to linger in everyone's mind about why we haven't used it on this SCP, or that SCP, and so on. If it only had a limited number of uses, or gave unreliable information regarding anomalous materials, or had some terrible downside that made people reluctant to use it, then it would be fine. As it stands, it's game over. The Foundation literally has the key to understanding everything, demolishing what makes SCP's special as a result.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I had an idea a while ago when I first started this of an SCP-1402-2, which would be a pen that, when you make markings on the documents, it alters that person's memory of it. Say, if I were to cross out the part of a D-Class' document that describes his crimes and conviction, they wouldn't be able to remember that they ever committed a crime in the first place.
Does that seem like a good idea?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. This is an excellent idea. Well, both are excellent ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To make that variant a little creepier: The variations never contradict the person's memories. Anything they've forgotten or had amnesiac'd is fair game. (A little background research can confirm the variations as inaccurate, of course.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what would make this better? If it wasn't always right. It gets rid of the "way too useful" part, and doesn't make it any less anomalous: It still summons documents from nowhere and still manages to accurately display some aspects of the subject's life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like the testlog. However, I do like this a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some things are meant to be used. I don't see a problem with this. The Foundation is surely allowed to use the things it comes across, especially if they're actually, y'know, useful. They use the clone tree and the clone coffin, don't they? And this is far less useful then those. Plus, as stated, it's dangerous in the wrong hands. Knowledge is power. I'll upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure I'd add that as O5 security is above and beyond (it has to be). The danger to the O5 would most likely come from a person interacting with an O5 that didn't know (bumped into them at the office or something.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you plenty, Boa, that's exactly what I was going for: This could be really bad for the Foundation in the wrong hands.
I'm considering adding an addendum where a member of the GOC attempted to get info on an O5 by stealing 1402 and intercepting a video stream betwixt the O5s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't want it because I have no need for a biography of myself, not to mention that the only reason a person would want this, aside from narcissistic purposes, is because they're paranoid.

It's not to use as a lazy backstory generator, it'd be for flavour text. For instance there are dozens if not hundreds of items on the wiki that have no discernible origin, they just are, and that works for some items but to me it's much more lazy than adding in a hint of origins. That's not even mentioning that any backstory made through 1402 would need to be written and created just as any bit of information on this site (hint hint: there is no SCP backstory generator machines that spit out useful subject specific information).

It's entirely lazy to judge an object on how useful it is for the reason that there are plenty of useful objects on the site (like the panacea pills, the tiny homunculus, 343, 914, the make anything tree, and many more).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would you not want it? The thing is dead useful and has no downside.

I'd also look down on anything using this to answer questions because it's a really lazy way of doing it. Sure, we could have the Foundation obtain information through actual investigation and experimentation… or we could just open the magic box of answers and get the answers for free. It's a cheap way of writing backstory in the same way that Telekill was a cheap way of writing containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does it need a stupid downside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Who cares if it's "useful"

I have never gotten this criticism. Heck, it makes the Foundation even more morally ambiguous if they DO contain things that are pretty freaking useful. Stuff that would benefit a lot of people, but they keep it from the world because it works weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does it HAVE to be useful? I'd LOVE 914, granted it could kill you if misused, but still. I don't see a reason things can't just be odd or strange. SCPs are things that need to be removed from society for one reason or another.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this as is, keep it simple, keep it direct. There is no need to clutter it with pointless limitations or trivial details. This is an item that could severely fuck up the O5 and at the same time provide some flavour text.

Is it powerful and useful? Sure is. Do I want it? Not a chance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too powerful, too useful.

Cut the information produced down immensely. 1000 pages? Making it produce 1 succinct page would be more interesting.

As for a downside, have it produce incorrect information half the time. "Is Dr. █████ conspiring with the Serpent's Hand? Who knows, but this thing was right about D-42731…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^ My thoughts exactly.

On another note, why do you mention the key that wasn't found if the briefcase can be opened anyway? It feels like you wanted to go somewhere with that and then just abandoned it mid-thought.

Holding my vote for now, in hopes of some more edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want this.

That's not a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said last time, this is one of those great head canon building SCPs that are lying around if you need them or want to use them. I predict many a story/experiment log will have this in them. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…And the conveniently untranslatable document has become a conveniently unexplained memetic hazard. Sorry, still doesn't work for me, and still doesn't break the "it's too darn useful" problem.

If I may make a suggestion: What if it has a limited supply of paper? The Foundation can only get so many biographies from it before it becomes useless. 10,000 sheets would be about right, given the rate of usage you've described, although that would require either unreasonably thin paper or a TARDIS-like interior. Alternatively, have it start with a plausible 1000-2000 sheets and make the individual biographies shorter. After using it a few times and counting the remaining sheets, the Foundation would be reluctant to use it much more, especially if they couldn't tell in advance how many sheets any one test would use up. That works even better if you remove the 1000 page limit.

(If you do that, I'd suggest either restoring the original untranslatable version of the 682 test, which isn't so obviously contrived if 1402 can't be used many more times, or have them still trying to decide whether it's worth risking a test with 682.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is good and fairly well executed, but I agree with other commenters that this is a bit too useful, without any real drawbacks other than the cheap get-out clause of a conveniently untranslatable document, to be a good SCP. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple little safe object. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should downvote on the princaple I've had an SCP sandboxed that was almost exactly like this, but I won't. Instead I'll stay neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaaand fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm… I'm going to stay neutral for now. It's an interesting concept, definitely. However, it seems incredibly useful to Foundation staff, whereas generally things have at least some drawback. I do like that it produces results when given to an object, though.
One other thing that bothers me is the experiment log; you've got six entries in there and three of them are SCP cross-tests. Even if you don't agree that cross-testing should be verboten, that's a very high ratio. (Although I do like the little tease you played with SCP-500.)
So yeah, no vote for now, we'll see what time and polishing does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, it seems way too useful to the Foundation, without any drawbacks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Change or delete the "Agent found it on the bus" bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like your attitude.

However, testing on other SCPs

They're referred to as 'SCP Objects'.

SCP-1402 was discovered on 07/20/20██ by Agent ████ while she traveled by the local city commute.

I personally dislike when an Agent randomly runs into an SCP. It shows to me that the writer just shoved it in. We don't need to know where it came from, especially if it's something so handwaven like this.

The article doesn't have a hook and reads rather boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Possible fix: show it to one SCP, resulting in [REDACTED] blood everywhere oh god the screaming [DATA EXPUNGED], followed by an executive order not to do that again.

Similarly, personnel exposure could be canceled after a memetic threat breaks out. I could imagine that the Foundation uses amnesiacs like hand sanitizer, and this is why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I were the Foundation, and this happened to show up randomly next to an agent on their drive to work one day, I would probably have a different reaction than "fiddle-dee-dee let's show it to ALL our personnel!".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm intrigued by this one; the only thing holding me back is the recovery story. No offense, but I hate it when SCPs are just randomly found by the Foundation and its personnel. If that were changed or omitted, I'd be on board. Also, I agree with Lireci: this one might be a bit too useful in explaining SCPs. You can only say "we got a story, but it's in a language/dialect no one knows" so many times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this could be really interesting, but right now it just seems too useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really not sure why, but I like this.
